narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and personal factors. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 1, an LSAT score of 36, an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, and plans to attend full-time—solid foundations in many areas, though below some averages like the typical 3.21 GPA or higher decile rankings. The model estimates a 47.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with strong confidence. The primary reason for this decision was your Year 3 decile ranking, which stood out as the most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure, since it was notably lower than the average of around 5.5—suggesting challenges in maintaining performance later in your studies. Counterbalancing this, your LSAT score had the strongest positive pull toward passing, performing close to the typical 36.7 average and showing real promise in logical reasoning skills. Your family income bracket also helped nudge the odds in favor of success, aligning with patterns of stability among admitted students. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA offered some support toward passing but wasn't as impactful, given it fell below the dataset average. Your Year 1 decile and full-time status provided minor boosts too, as full-time students make up over 90% of those who succeed. Compared to typical students who pass, your later-year grades were the biggest outlier dragging the prediction down, while your test scores held you closer to the pack. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive background—many with similar profiles succeed with targeted effort. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or prep programs, retake the LSAT if possible to push past 36, and consider part-time options or gap-year experience to demonstrate growth. Reapply after addressing these, and we encourage you to seek advising from pre-law counselors for personalized strategies. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing.
